questions.q: What kind of movement was Romanticism?
questions.feedback: Romanticism is primarily categorized as a literary and artistic movement that emphasized aesthetics and individualism.

questions.q: Romanticism as a movement placed the greatest emphasis on
questions.feedback: A hallmark of Romanticism is the focus on intense emotion and personal experience over rationalism.

questions.q: (...) was essential, and it could only be achieved through subjectivity
questions.feedback: Originality was considered essential in Romantic thought, reflecting the artist's unique subjective vision.

questions.q: Which of the following had a minimal influence on British romanticism?
questions.feedback: While present, nationalism was often secondary to the deep focus on spirituality and the supernatural in British Romantic literature.

questions.q: The Age of enlightenment (...) Romanticism
questions.feedback: The Age of Enlightenment generally preceded the Romantic era, serving as the rationalistic period that Romantics reacted against.

questions.q: Which was a proto-Romantic movement?
questions.feedback: The German 'Sturm und Drang' (Storm and Drive) movement is widely recognized as a precursor to Romanticism.

questions.q: (...) lasted through the sixteenth, seventeenth, and eighteenth centuries
questions.feedback: The Scientific Revolution spanned roughly from the 16th through the 18th centuries, fundamentally shifting the worldview of the era.

questions.q: How many Reform Acts were introduced in Britain during the 19th century?
questions.feedback: There were two major Reform Acts (1832 and 1867) that significantly altered the British electoral system in the 19th century.

questions.q: Lord Byron's 'Darkness' was composed using:
questions.feedback: Lord Byron's 'Darkness' is notable for being written in blank verse, which is unrhymed iambic pentameter.

questions.q: Lord Byron's 'Childe Harold's Pilgrimage' consists of:
questions.feedback: The completed work of 'Childe Harold's Pilgrimage' is famously structured into four cantos.

questions.q: The poem's tone is best described as:
questions.feedback: The tone of much of Byron's Childe Harold is reflective and melancholic, typical of the Byronic hero.

questions.q: The central theme of Wordsworth's 'Mutability' is:
questions.feedback: Wordsworth's 'Mutability' explores the inevitability of change, presenting it as a natural and ultimately noble truth of the world.

questions.q: Wordsworth suggests that all but one of the following do not realise that nothing lasts forever:
questions.feedback: The poem suggests that power and high status often lead individuals (like kings) to erroneously believe they are exempt from the ravages of time.

questions.q: Shelley's 'Hymn to Intellectual Beauty' is a praise of:
questions.feedback: The poem is an invocation and praise of the 'spirit of beauty' or intellectual beauty that provides inspiration to the poet.

questions.q: Shelley mentioned that he sought for the truth of intellectual beauty in:
questions.feedback: Shelley sought it in a 'listening chamber, cave and ruin, / And starlight wood'. He actively rebelled against the Church.

questions.q: As a poem of praise and prayer, its form and style reflect that of:
questions.feedback: Given its elevated subject matter and lyrical, prayer-like address to an abstract concept, it is classically structured as an ode.

questions.q: In his poem, 'Time', Shelley personifies time as:
questions.feedback: Shelley often depicts time as a powerful force that simultaneously destroys the old and creates the new.

questions.q: When Shelley said that Time's 'waves are years', he compares it to:
questions.feedback: The metaphor of 'waves' suggests the relentless, tumultuous, and unending movement characteristic of a stormy ocean.

questions.q: Shelley suggests Time's power is:
questions.feedback: Shelley portrays time as an absolute force that governs all earthly things, immune to human intervention.

questions.q: The poem's tone is best described as:
questions.feedback: The reflection on the unstoppable and grave nature of time makes the tone of the poem inherently serious.

questions.q: Who was considered the father of English Romanticism during his time?
questions.feedback: William Wordsworth is widely considered a foundational figure and the 'father' of the English Romantic movement due to his Lyrical Ballads.

questions.q: Which Romantic poet worked in restoring churches?
questions.feedback: Wordsworth had an affinity for the preservation of historical church structures.

questions.q: What is the common characteristic in the poetry of all Romantic poets that cannot be controlled?
questions.feedback: The Romantic concept of Imagination is often described as a wild, innate, and uncontrollable creative force.

questions.q: Which of the following poems is not a sonnet?
questions.feedback: While many Romantic poems are sonnets, Shelley's 'Time' does not follow the strict 14-line sonnet structure.

questions.q: Thy spells did bind/ To fear himself, and love all human kind is a line from Shelley's:
questions.feedback: This line is a distinctive part of Shelley's 'Hymn to Intellectual Beauty'.

questions.q: Blake's 'Songs of Innocence and of Experience' mainly represented themes about:
questions.feedback: Blake's dual collection explores the complexities of human nature through the contrasting states of innocence and experience.

questions.q: Who described himself as 'a visionary poet'?
questions.feedback: Shelley explicitly describes himself saying, 'I am a visionary poet,' while describing Wordsworth as 'a natural poet.'
